  • Tutorial: Wall Insulation Scenarios
  • Tutorial: Wall Insulation Scenarios

Tutorial: Wall Insulation Scenarios

GreenhousesJL models 4 independent cardinal wall nodes (N, S, E, W). Each wall has its own temperature state, area, emissivity, insulation U-value, thermal mass, and sky view factor.

Default: all glass

using GreenhousesJL

tmy = load_tmy("path/to/tmy.txt")

# Savona 5×5 m with all-glass walls
p = SavonaParams(tmy; with_walls=true)
# → p.walls is GlassWalls(L=5, W=5, h_gutter=2, rise=2.1)
# → 4 glass walls: N=10 m², S=10 m², E=15.4 m², W=15.4 m²

Insulating specific walls

Use the insulate function to modify any wall:

# Start with glass walls
w = GlassWalls(; L=15.3, W=9.9, h_gutter=3.5)

# Insulate the north wall (common energy-saving strategy)
w = insulate(w, :N; U=0.4, ε=0.90, thermal_mass=50000.0)

# Insulate north + east
w = insulate(w, [:N, :E]; U=0.3)

# Polycarbonate on all walls
w = insulate(w, :all; U=2.5, ε=0.85)

Parameters

ParameterDescriptionTypical values
UInsulation U-value [W/(m²K)]0.3-0.5 (foam/mineral wool), 2.5 (polycarbonate), 6.0 (single glass)
εSurface emissivity0.84 (glass), 0.90 (opaque wall), 0.05 (low-e glass)
thermal_massExtra thermal capacity [J/(m²·K)]0 (foam panels), 50000 (massive concrete/brick)

Wall indices

IndexCardinalTypical use
1 (:N)NorthMost common insulation target (no direct sun in NH)
2 (:S)SouthMaximum solar gain, usually kept as glass
3 (:E)East (gable)Morning sun
4 (:W)West (gable)Afternoon sun

Comparing scenarios

# Define base geometry
base = GlassWalls(; L=15.3, W=9.9, h_gutter=3.5)

# Scenario 1: all glass (reference)
p1 = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=151.5, walls=base, Kp=100*151.5, ...)

# Scenario 2: insulated north wall
north_ins = insulate(base, :N; U=0.4, thermal_mass=50000.0)
p2 = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=151.5, walls=north_ins, ...)

# Scenario 3: Chinese massive north wall
chinese = insulate(base, :N; U=0.3, ε=0.90, thermal_mass=50000.0)
p3 = GreenhouseParams(tmy; A=151.5, walls=chinese, ...)

# Run all three and compare Q_heat

Per-wall sky view factors

The sky view factor determines how much LW radiation each wall loses to the cold sky. In a box-shaped greenhouse, adjacent walls and the roof partially block each other's sky view:

WallF_skyRationale
N0.35Faces away from equator, more open sky
S0.25Faces horizon, partially blocked by buildings/vegetation
E0.30Intermediate
W0.30Intermediate

These values are calibrated so the average (0.30) matches the effective view factor for a compact greenhouse box geometry, consistent with EnergyPlus per-surface calculations.

Energy balance per wall

Each wall k ∈ {N, S, E, W} solves:

C_k × dT_wall_k/dt = Q_cnv_air_wall_k     (inside convection, from air)
                    + Q_rad_flr_wall_k      (interior LW from floor)
                    + Q_rad_cov_wall_k      (interior LW from cover)
                    − Q_cnv_wall_out_k      (exterior convection, Walton 1981)
                    − Q_rad_wall_sky_k      (exterior LW to sky)
                    − Q_rad_wall_grd_k      (exterior LW to ground)

The total air-side wall loss is:

Q_cnv_air_wall = Σ_k h_in_k × A_k × (T_air − T_wall_k)

Reference

  • Walton, G.N. (1981). Passive solar heating analysis. ASHRAE Transactions, 87(1).
  • Kusuda, T. & Achenbach, P.R. (1965). Earth temperature and thermal diffusivity. NBS Report 8972.
  • Fossa, M. (2023). Solar and Geothermal Energy. UniGe DIME course notes.
